name: Well of Mimir
description: The Well of Mimir, also known as Mímisbrunnr, is a sacred well in Norse mythology associated with wisdom and knowledge. It is located beneath one of the roots of the world tree Yggdrasil, in the realm of Jotunheim. Mimir, a being of immense wisdom, guards the well. The water from the well is said to grant profound understanding and foresight to those who drink from it. Odin, the Allfather, famously sacrificed one of his eyes to Mimir in exchange for a single drink from the well, demonstrating the immense value placed on the well's wisdom. This sacrifice highlights the Norse emphasis on seeking knowledge, even at great personal cost. The well is a source of counsel and foresight, and its waters are believed to hold the secrets of the universe. The Well of Mimir represents the constant pursuit of wisdom and the price one might pay to obtain it. It is a pivotal location in several myths, symbolizing the depth of knowledge and the interconnectedness of all things within the Norse cosmos. The well's location at the root of Yggdrasil further emphasizes its connection to the fundamental structure of the world and its influence on the fate of gods and humans alike. Mimir's presence ensures that the well's wisdom is guarded and only accessible to those who are willing to make a significant offering.
subtitle: Source of Wisdom and Sacrifice

keyMyths.title: Odin's Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: Odin sacrifices one of his eyes to Mimir in exchange for a single drink from the Well of Mimir, granting him immense wisdom and foresight. This act symbolizes the value placed on knowledge and the sacrifices one must make to obtain it.

keyMyths.title: Mimir's Head
keyMyths.description: After the Aesir-Vanir War, Mimir was beheaded. Odin preserved Mimir's head and used his magic to ensure it could still speak, seeking counsel from Mimir's wisdom even after death.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A key source for Norse mythology, containing stories about the gods, creation, and Ragnarok.
sources.text: A key source for Norse mythology, containing stories about the gods, creation, and Ragnarok.

sources.author: Various Skalds
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems, some of which reference Mimir and the Well of Mimir.
